          {
            "lineNumber": 874,
            "text": "    def get_league_season_cumulated(self, league_name: str, season: str, week: int) -\u003e pd.DataFrame:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 875,
            "text": "        \"\"\""
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 876,
            "text": "        High level database query."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 877,
            "text": "        Composes a table that represents a league's results in a given season, cumulated up to a specific week."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 878
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 879,
            "text": "        Args:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 880,
            "text": "            league_name (str): The name of the league."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 881,
            "text": "            season (str): The season."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 882,
            "text": "            week (int): The week number up to which to cumulate."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 883
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 884,
            "text": "        Returns:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 885,
            "text": "            pd.DataFrame: The cumulated league standings for the given season up to the specified week."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 886,
            "text": "        \"\"\""
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 887,
            "text": "        columns_to_fetch = [Columns.team_name, Columns.points, Columns.score, Columns.week]"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 888,
            "text": "        "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 889,
            "text": "        # Use the new filter format"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 890,
            "text": "        filters_individual = {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 891,
            "text": "            Columns.league_name: {'value': league_name, 'operator': 'eq'},"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 892,
            "text": "            Columns.season: {'value': season, 'operator': 'eq'},"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 893,
            "text": "            Columns.week: {'value': week, 'operator': 'le'},"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 894,
            "text": "            Columns.computed_data: {'value': False, 'operator': 'eq'}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 895,
            "text": "        }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 896,
            "text": "        "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 897,
            "text": "        filters_team = {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 898,
            "text": "            Columns.league_name: {'value': league_name, 'operator': 'eq'},"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 899,
            "text": "            Columns.season: {'value': season, 'operator': 'eq'},"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 900,
            "text": "            Columns.week: {'value': week, 'operator': 'le'},"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 901,
            "text": "            Columns.computed_data: {'value': True, 'operator': 'eq'}"

          {

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I see a critical bug on lines 1448-1449. The code compares an integer `week` (from `get_latest_week`) with floats in `all_weeks` (from dataframe). While Python allows comparison between int 18 and float 18.0, this may cause issues in other contexts or lead to unexpected behavior. The logic correctly handles single week mode and history mode differently, but the type mismatch should be addressed for consistency and clarity.

- Line 1490 filters weeks where `w <= week` 
- Line 1492 sets weeks to show as `[week]` when not including history
- For history mode, it uses list comprehension to filter all weeks up to current week</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
If `get_available_weeks` returns an empty list, the issue may stem from incorrect filter keys. If `get_available_weeks` returns an empty list due to a filter bug, `get_latest_week` still returns 1, leading to `weeks_to_show = [1.0]` and only displaying one week of data in the league table.

This results in an "almost empty" table with either:
- Very few columns (only first week data)
- Possibly no rows (teams) if filtering affects team data too

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Line 215 contains a bug where `league_name=` should be corrected to `league=`. In Python 3, the error `TypeError: Server.get_weeks() got an unexpected keyword argument 'league_name'` occurs because line 215 incorrectly uses `league_name=` instead of `league=`. This bug causes `get_league_history` to crash when `week` is `None`, but `get_league_history_table_data` calls `get_league_table_simple` rather than `get_league_history` directly.

The flow is:
- `league_routes get_league_history`  `league_service.get_league_history_table_data`  `get_league_table_simple`

Therefore, **season overview uses `get_league_table_simple`, not server's `get_league_history`**. The buggy line 215 may be unused in this UI path.

Key points about `get_league_table_simple`:
- Uses `get_latest_week` which relies on adapter. get_weeks(season, league) returns the maximum week number as an integer. If the adapter correctly provides week data, the week value will be a proper integer.

Line 1486 sorts unique weeks from league data, which may contain numpy floats like 18.0 instead of integers.

Line 1490 filters weeks to show only those less than or equal to the current week, so if week is 18 (int) and all_weeks ends at np.float64(18.0), it works correctly.

Potential issues arise in column construction around line 1570 where column keys might use float values instead of integers, possibly causing problems in JSON serialization or frontend handling due to duplicate keys such as 18 and 18.0. Actually, "table almost empty" might mean **few columns** (only week 1) if `week` is incorrectly set to a small number.

Week could be 1 if:
- `get_available_weeks` returns an empty list  `get_latest_week` defaults to 1
- Then `weeks_to_show = [w for w in all_weeks if w <= 1]` results in only `[1.0]`

Why would `get_weeks` return empty?
- `get_filtered_data__deprecated` with Season and League fails to match league string (due to encoding or spacing issues)  filtered data is empty  `get_weeks` returns empty list  adapter returns empty data Let me read the adapter's `get_weeks` again  if `result` is empty from `get_filtered_data__deprecated`, it proceeds to `get_weeks` with that result.

Actually:
```python
def get_weeks(self, season: str, league: str) -> List[int]:
    filters = {"Season": season, "League": league}
    result = self.get_filtered_data__deprecated(filters_eq=filters)
    
    if result.empty:
        return []
    
    if "Week" in result.columns:
```

If `result` is the full dataframe when the empty check is performed  but if filters match nothing, `result` might still be non-empty but with zero rows. The issue arises if filtering does not reduce rows, meaning `result` remains non-empty even when no matches exist. If no rows match season+league, the empty result leads to an empty list being returned. However, if `get_filtered_data` (new API) with the same season and league returns data, then `get_weeks` should not be empty.

Unless `get_filtered_data__deprecated` uses exact matching (`==`) while `get_filtered_data` uses a lambda function with an operator (`apply(lambda x: op_func(x, value))`), causing different behavior with NaN values or type coercion.

In that case, even if `individual_data` is not empty, `all_weeks` would still contain the weeks. `get_latest_week` continues to call the adapter. get_weeks may return an empty list if the deprecated strict equality filter on League doesn't match due to trailing spaces or encoding issues.

This mismatch between `get_filtered_data` (new) and `get_filtered_data__deprecated` (old) can cause:
- league_data to contain rows (new filter works)
- get_latest_week to return 1 when weeks=[] 
- weeks_to_show to only include weeks <= 1

The filtering difference is:
- Line 241 deprecated: `filtered_df[column] == value` (strict equality)
- Line 148 new: `result[column].apply(lambda x: op_func(x, value))` (with operator function)</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I found a critical bug in the `get_league_table_simple` function. The bug involves inconsistent data handling where week values are floats (e.g., 1.0) in backend processing, leading to column field names like "week1.0_points" instead of "week1_points". This inconsistency occurs when creating table data and could cause frontend issues, as the frontend expects "week1_points" but receives "week1.0_points". The problem stems from how weeks are processed in `get_league_table_simple`, particularly in lines 1547, 1596-1603, and 1626, where the week number format is not normalized. Row extensions correctly use the same week value from `team_info['weekly_data'][w]`.

The frontend's `createTableBootstrap3` function searches for field names like `week1_points` but receives `week1.0_points` due to this inconsistency. When building table rows, the code uses float week numbers (like 1.0) as dictionary keys in `weekly_data`, resulting in column field names such as `week1.0_points`. However, the frontend expects integer-based keys like `week1_points`, causing a mismatch that leads to empty cells. This happens because JSON serialization converts float keys to strings, and JavaScript/JSON parsing may not align these keys properly, preventing correct data binding in the table renderer. Normalize week values to integers throughout the pipeline:  
- After `_load_data`, cast the `Week` column to nullable Int64.  
- In `get_league_table_simple`, ensure all week-related operations use integer keys.  

Specifically:  
- Convert `all_weeks` to integers after computing them: `all_weeks_int = sorted({int(float(x)) for x in all_weeks if pd.notna(x)})`  
- Use consistent integer formatting for `weeks_to_show`, `weekly_data` keys, and field names like `week{n}_points`  
- Ensure comparisons and data access use integer week values to avoid mismatches between backend and frontend expectations. To fix the inconsistency, normalize week values to integers throughout the pipeline. Use a helper function `_norm_week` to convert float weeks to integers, ensuring consistent handling in `weekly_data` keys and field names. Apply this normalization to `all_weeks` from unique values and `weeks_to_show` when including history, maintaining integer formatting for all week-related operations. This prevents mismatches between backend and frontend expectations. weeks_to_show is filtered to include only weeks up to the specified week integer.

For inner loop filtering, the code compares against normalized week values using `_norm_week` or direct comparison.

The cleanest approach is to normalize the Week column once during data loading in `DataAdapterPandas._load_data` by converting it to numeric and optionally rounding to nullable integers.

This ensures consistent handling of week values throughout the pipeline.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The table relies on array positional data, meaning field names do not influence cell binding when using createTableBootstrap3. Then why "almost empty"?

Re-analyze `weeks_to_show = [w for w in all_weeks if w <= week]`:
- If `all_weeks` contains floats `np.float64(18.0)` and `week` is `18` (int), comparisons OK.
- If `week` comes from `get_latest_week` when `get_available_weeks` returns `[]` -> `week = 1`
- `all_weeks` could be `[1.0, 2.0, ..., 18.0]` from data
- `weeks_to_show = [1.0]` only - **ONE week column** - table looks "almost empty" compared to full 18 columns!

So the fix is ensuring **get_available_weeks** returns non-empty when data exists. That means `adapter.` get_weeks must not return empty when data exists.

Why would get_weeks return empty with deprecated filter?
- Season string mismatch: URL has "25/26" but CSV has "25/26" with different unicode
- League mismatch

OR wrong argument order in Server - that's get_weeks on server calling adapter.get_weeks(season, league) - correct.

DataAdapter get_weeks uses `get_filtered_data__deprecated(filters_eq={"Season": season, "League": league})`.

`get_filtered_data` (new) in get_league_table_simple uses `Columns.league_name` which equals `'League'` and `Columns.season` = 'Season' - same keys.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The week parameter consistently returns None in the get_league_history path because get_latest_week extracts an integer from the maximum weeks value. When filtering team_week_individual, comparing float64 values like 1.0 with integers like 1 returns True due to numpy's element-wise comparison. To resolve this, normalize all week-related fields to integers during data loading and processing. Specifically:
- Normalize `week` to int in get_league_table_simple
- Normalize `all_weeks` to sorted set of ints
- Ensure consistent type handling across all code paths

This prevents:
- Field names being incorrectly formatted as week1.0_*
- Edge cases involving mixed data types
- Inconsistent comparisons between float and int values

Also fix the server bug related to league_name. For `get_filtered_data`, add tolerance when comparing Week columns with int values using numeric coercion. However, prefer column coercion at load time instead.

Implement `_normalize_week_series` in data_adapter_pandas as a method called from `_load_data` and `set_dataframe`.

- Normalize week column to int type during data loading
- Handle case where dataframe or week column is missing
- Ensure consistent week data types across all operations Convert the week column to Int64 type using `to_numeric` with `errors="coerce"` followed by rounding and casting to ensure consistent integer handling. This prevents issues when comparing week values with floats, as pandas may treat `Int64(5)` and `5.0` as equal in comparisons. The risk of inconsistent behavior arises from potential float vs. integer comparisons in filters, but direct equality checks like `3 == 3` remain reliable.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Normalizing the `Week` column after CSV load (floats like `1.0` → integer) and coercing week values in `get_league_table_simple` so comparisons and `week{n}_*` field names stay consistent. Fixing `Server.get_league_history`'s incorrect `get_weeks(league_name=...)` keyword (would break paths that use `week=None`).

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="edit_file_v2">

<details><summary>Tool use: **edit_file_v2** • Edit file: c:\Users\cfell\OneDrive\Documents\001_repositories\league_analyzer\league_analyzer_v1\app\services\league_service.py</summary>

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="edit_file_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **edit_file_v2**</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

Here is what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Cause
Pipeline CSVs often end up with **`Week` as float** (`1.0`, `2.0`, …) after `read_csv`. That leads to:

1. **`get_league_table_simple`** building dynamic fields like `week1.0_points` / titles like “Week 1.0”, which is fragile and easy to misalign with anything that assumes integer weeks.
2. **Risky mixes** of `int` (e.g. from `get_latest_week`) and `float` in `<=` / `==` filters on raw frames (and one **real bug**: `get_league_history` called `get_weeks(league_name=…)` even though the parameter is `league`, so that path could fail or behave oddly depending on version).

### Fixes
1. **`DataAdapterPandas`** – After every load (`_load_data`) and when a frame is injected (`set_dataframe`), **`Week` is coerced** with `to_numeric` → `round(0)` → **`Int64`** so stored values are integer-like and filters use consistent types.

2. **`LeagueService.get_league_table_simple`** – After resolving the active week, **`week = int(week)`**, and **`all_weeks`** is built as a sorted set of **`int(round(float(w)))`** so week lists and per-week logic never depend on `1.0` vs `1`.

3. **`get_team_points_simple` / `get_team_averages_simple`** – The same **integer week list** logic so season charts and averages stay aligned with the table.

4. **`Server.get_league_history`** – **`get_weeks(league=league_name, season=season)`** and **`max(weeks) if weeks else 1`** so the keyword is correct and an empty week list does not blow up.

After redeploy/restart, the season overview table and related charts should fill using integer week indexing end-to-end. If you ever need **non-integer** match days, we would narrow the coercion (e.g. only round when the fractional part is ~0) instead of always rounding.

---

_**User**_

alright, fixed it!
So, lets focus on the server load when fetching data updates. 
can you add a benchmark in the run_gf_pipeline script to compare the time needed to figure out whether new data is available vs the time needed to sync the table. put the times and some metadata in the log files 

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user wants to benchmark:
- Benchmark 1
- Benchmark 2
- Benchmark 3 The user wants to benchmark three specific items: Benchmark 1, Benchmark 2, and Benchmark 3.

Regarding the time to check for new data availability, this involves determining if there's new data through an incremental fetch API call. In the current implementation, `client.fetch_incremental_entries(...)` serves dual purposes: discovering new data and returning entries simultaneously. There's no separate HEAD or check operation in the code. Therefore, measuring the time for this function would include both checking for new data and downloading new rows. The key consideration is whether to measure the entire `fetch_incremental_entries` call or isolate just the data discovery portion. The user wants to distinguish between two phases: Phase 1, which involves discovering whether new data is available (potentially through a fast API call like a HEAD request or count query), and Phase 2, which involves syncing the table by merging and writing legacy data. Currently, the `fetch_incremental_entries` function performs both tasks simultaneously. The user should explore if the GF API offers a separate lightweight endpoint for checking data availability without fetching the actual entries. Alternatively:
- **"detect new data"** = time for the incremental HTTP request(s) until we know `len(incoming_entries)` (the whole fetch_incremental_entries is the "availability check" in terms of network - but sync is local processing).
- **"sync the table"** = sanitize, write csv, `_run_gf_canonical_legacy` (merge + legacy write)

Or split as:
- **Availability**: `fetch_incremental_entries` only (or for full mode, first page only to see if empty?)

For **incremental**, a reasonable split:
- `t_discover` = time inside `GfClient.` `fetch_incremental_entries` includes both network transfer and parsing to retrieve the entry listessentially "getting updates from the API."  
- `t_sync` = time after entries are received via `state.save` and `_run_gf_canonical_legacy` (staging, sanitization, merge, and legacy write).  

The user may also want to measure a lighter operation:  
- Time for an API call that simply checks for updates (e.g., first response with count).  

Ill examine `gf_client` for incremental fetch to see if pagination allows timing just the "first page" versus the restthough that approach seems awkward. - **`t_fetch_updates`** = time spent in `fetch_incremental_entries` or full pagination loop  remote I/O to fetch new/changed entries.
- **`t_sync_local`** = time for rows_from_entries, sanitize, write_csv, _run_gf_canonical_legacy, state.save  processing and writing merged/legacy table. - **Availability check** involves comparing `last_seen_date_updated` locally versus the first API roundtrip, which either returns 0 or N entries. Since the incremental API returns entries directly, theres no separate "check" in the current code.

Implemented:
- **`bench_fetch_ms`** / **`fetch_new_data_seconds`**: Measures elapsed time for the Gravity Forms HTTP fetch retrieving candidate updates (via `fetch_incremental_entries` or full-mode page loop). **bench_sync_ms** / **sync_table_seconds** measures the elapsed time for processing fetched data, excluding the initial fetch phase. This includes `rows_from_entries`, `sanitize_rows`, `write_csv` (x2), `state.save`, and `_run_gf_canonical_legacy`.

- **Discovery / fetch updates**: `client.fetch_*` only
- **Sync table**: `rows_from_entries` + `sanitize_rows` + `write_csv` x2 + `state` + `_run_gf_canonical_legacy` I'll use JSON names `benchmark_seconds_fetch_updates` and `benchmark_seconds_sync_table`, storing metadata like form_id, mode, and counts in the log manifest.

I'll use `time.perf_counter()` for high-resolution timing.

Implementation plan:
- In `runner.py` `run_ingest`, for each form:
  - Record start time
  - Perform fetch block (incremental or full loop)
  - Calculate fetch time
  - Record start time
  - Perform rows processing, sanitization, writes, state update, and legacy sync
  - Calculate sync time - Add benchmark results to the `form_result` dictionary and merge them into the run-level summary in the manifest.
- Include a top-level `benchmark` section with metadata such as `python_version` and `platform` from `sys`.
- Metadata to include:
  - `run_id`, `mode`, `form_id`
  - `entries_fetched`, `staged_rows`, `sanitized_rows`
  - `merged_row_count` / `legacy_row_count` from the legacy result subdictionary
  - `incremental` vs `full` mode
  - SSL verify flag from config (if needed, may require passing or reading from `GfConfig`)  
- For full mode, the fetch phase includes all pages, which is correct for retrieving updates; sync remains local. When incremental fetch returns zero entries quickly, the fetch time is still meaningful as a probe, and sync still processes empty files and runs the legacy refresh path.

To clarify the two phases:
- **Check**: `fetch_incremental_entries` (network operation)
- **Sync**: `_run_gf_canonical_legacy` (legacy/latest.csv processing)

Timing for `_run_gf_canonical_legacy` will be separated into the legacy dictionary, while "sync table" will encompass all post-fetch processing.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Incremental fetch retrieves pages from the API until either the result set is empty or it encounters entries with date_updated less than or equal to the 'since' parameter, at which point it stops adding new entries but still performs network requests for those final pages.
- Fetches pages incrementally from the API
- Continues until empty result or date threshold reached
- Stops adding entries when date condition met
- Still makes network calls for final pages even if not added The "figure out whether new data is available" refers to determining if any entries are newer than the 'since' parameter, which can be assessed by examining only the first page fetch. If the first page is empty, no new data exists; if it contains only stale entries, one page suffices.

Benchmarking includes measuring:
- Time to determine if new data is available
- Time to sync the entire table

For incremental fetch, `seconds_probe_new_data` is defined as the time until we know whether entries exist newer than 'since'. This can be measured by timing only the first `fetch_entries_page` call within `fetch_incremental_entries`, though this is inside GfClient. A cleaner approach is to add a new method `fetch_incremental_probe` or time the operation in the runner by splitting client functionality.

The simplest approach without significantly changing the gf_client API involves:

- Option 1: Adding optional profiling inside `GfClient.fetch_incremental_entries` that returns a tuple of (entries, timing)  this would be invasive.
- Option 2: Duplicating the first page logic in the runner for incremental mode to time "first page" separately  this creates unwanted duplication. Option 3 modifies the return type of `fetch_incremental_entries` to include timing and page details, but breaks existing callers.

Option 4 introduces a new method `GfClient.fetch_incremental_entries_profiled` that returns structured data, avoiding breaking changes.

Option 5 keeps the user benchmark as `seconds_remote_fetch`, measuring the full duration of `fetch_incremental_entries` including the initial check for new data. - **`seconds_local_sync`**: time for legacy write after fetch
- **`seconds_fetch_until_first_page`**: time for first HTTP response in incremental ("probe")
- **`seconds_fetch_remaining_pages`**: time for remaining incremental pages
- **`seconds_sync_table`**: local sync time

Two-way split:
1. Time to probe for new data (first page)
2. Time to sync the table (local operations) - **Probe/fetch**: Time for all GF HTTP calls in the loop (`fetch_incremental_entries` or full while), labeled as `seconds_gravity_fetch` or `seconds_detect_and_fetch_updates`
- **Sync**: Local processing time, labeled as `seconds_sync_table`

Benchmark labels:
- `benchmark.new_data_detection_and_fetch_seconds`: Time for all GF client HTTP work to discover and retrieve new rows
- `benchmark.table_sync_seconds`: Time for local row processing from entries through legacy steps

If users want "check without download," API support would be required, which is not currently available in the codebase. The docstring in benchmark indicates that data detection is combined with incremental entry retrieval, as the GF API lacks a separate lightweight check.

A note will be added to benchmark.metadata.explanation.

To enable first-page-only timing, `fetch_incremental_entries` can be refactored to accept a callback or yield results, with a new method `fetch_incremental_entries_with_stats` added to `gf_client` that returns both entries and fetch statistics. IncrementalFetchStats includes fields for first-page and total-page timing. The runner sets:
- `seconds_new_data_probe` = stats.seconds_first_page
- `seconds_fetch_additional_pages` = stats.seconds_total - stats.seconds_first_page

User wants to compare "time to figure out whether new data is available" vs "time to sync the table."

Implementation plan:
1. Add `fetch_incremental_entries_with_stats` to `gf_client` returning both entries and fetch statistics
2. Refactor `fetch_incremental_entries` to support callbacks or yielding results
3. Update runner to use new method and set benchmark labels accordingly
4. Benchmark labels:
   - `seconds_gravity_fetch`: Time for all GF HTTP calls in incremental fetch
   - `seconds_sync_table`: Local processing time
5. Add note to benchmark.metadata.explanation about data detection being combined with fetch

No output **`seconds_first_entry_page`** - time for first `fetch_entries_page` in incremental (or first page in full)  proxy for "minimal work to see if updates exist"  
**`seconds_all_entry_fetch`** - total HTTP time for all pages (for incremental: entire fetch_incremental; requires instrumentation in gf_client)

"Figure out whether new data is available" refers to a cheap check: the first page is the right proxy because latest entries are sorted by date_updated; if the first entry's date_updated is less than or equal to the 'since' parameter, no new data exists. The current implementation continues fetching until it finds entries older than the threshold, rather than stopping immediately upon finding stale data. The first page provides the necessary information to determine if new data is available.

Instrument `fetch_incremental_entries` to capture timing for each `fetch_entries_page` call:
- Return `probe_seconds` for the first page only
- Return `fetch_total_seconds` as the sum of all page times

User can choose to measure:
1. Probe time (first page)
2. Sync time (local processing)

Labels to use:
- **`benchmark.seconds_entry_api_total`**  total GF entry HTTP time
- **`benchmark.seconds_table_sync_total`**  local sync time

Additionally, add:
- **`benchmark.seconds_entry_api_probe`**  time for first page only (data detection)
- **`benchmark.seconds_entry_api_fetch`**  time for remaining pages (full fetch) seconds_first_entry_page` represents the time for the first `fetch_entries_page` call, serving as the "probe" time to determine if new data exists.

User requested two specific comparisons:
- **`seconds_discover_new_data`** = time for the first `fetch_entries_page` call in incremental mode; for full mode, the first page of the full sync  labeled as "first page (includes detecting relative tail)"
- **`seconds_sync_table`** = total time for the local merge/write pipeline

For full mode, "discover new data" doesn't apply the same way since the entire multi-page fetch is considered the fetch operation. - **Incremental mode** measures `seconds_remote_entries_fetch` for the first HTTP page (data discovery) and `seconds_local_table_sync` for subsequent pages plus local processing.
- **Full mode** uses `seconds_remote_entries_fetch` for total HTTP time and `seconds_local_table_sync` for local sync operations.
- These labels clearly distinguish server-side fetch time from local table synchronization time. User wants to measure "figure out whether new data is available" as first page latency, with `seconds_first_page` as extra metadata, while keeping total remote fetch vs local sync as the main comparison.

Three metrics will be implemented in JSON:
- `seconds_first_entry_page`  time for first GF entries HTTP call (probe/early freshness inference)
- `seconds_all_entry_pages`  total GF entries HTTP time (includes incremental continuation pages) - `seconds_local_pipeline`  all post-last page operations: transform, CSV, state, legacy

User requested two comparisons:
1. `seconds_entry_fetch_total`  total API pages for entries (includes data discovery)
2. `seconds_table_sync_total`  local merge + latest + staging operations

Metadata includes `seconds_first_entry_page` as optional insight.

Timing in runner: refactor fetch into timed blocks without modifying `gf_client`, measuring first page separately for incremental mode using `client.fetch_entries_page(form_id, 1, ...)`.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Only the runner process makes calls to fetch incremental entries. I can change the return type to a dataclass or named tuple.

- Added `IncrementalFetchResult` dataclass in `gf_client.py`
- Includes `entries` and `page_timings_seconds` fields
- Adds properties for `seconds_first_page` and `seconds_all_pages`
- Maintains backward compatibility while providing structured return type Refactor `fetch_incremental_entries` to return `IncrementalFetchResult` instead of raw data, maintaining backward compatibility. Only the runner process calls this function.

For full mode loop, track per-page timings inside runner using `perf_counter` around `fetch_entries_page`.

Runner structure:
- Use `client.fetch_incremental_entries_profiled(...)`
- Extract `incoming_entries` and `page_timings` from result
- Name method `fetch_incremental_entries` returning `IncrementalFetchResult` for clarity - Collect page timings using `perf_counter()` around each `fetch_entries_page` call.
- Calculate total time as sum of all page timings.
- Extract first-page timing from the list of page timings.
- Measure local sync time separately using `perf_counter()`.
- Include benchmark data in the form result with first-page timing. - seconds_all_entry_fetch_pages: total time for all fetch pages
- seconds_local_table_sync: local sync time
- pages_fetched: number of pages fetched
- metadata includes form_id, mode, pipeline version, and sanitized site host
- first page timing mapped to new data probe
- all fetch timing mapped to second page timing User wants to compare the time to determine if new data is available versus the time to sync the table.

Log both first-page and total-fetch timings in metadata with these primary fields:
- `seconds_new_data_probe`: time for first HTTP page only (answers "after one roundtrip, do we already know freshness")
- `seconds_table_sync`: local pipeline sync time

Also log `seconds_entry_fetch_total` (all pages) for context, since incremental often requires multiple pages.

The key comparison is between:
1. `seconds_new_data_probe` (time to detect new data)
2. `seconds_table_sync` (local sync time) The key comparison is between the time to detect new data and the time to sync the table.

For incremental mode, the "new data detection" time is best represented by the first page fetch.
For full mode, since the first page doesn't indicate new data availability, the total time across all entry-fetch HTTP calls should represent the "fetch phase".

Neutral naming:
- `seconds_remote_data_fetch`: total time for fetching remote data
- `seconds_table_sync`: local pipeline sync time - **`seconds_remote_fetch_entries`**  total HTTP time to fetch all entry payloads for this form.
- **`seconds_local_sync_table`**  time for local table sync operations including transforms and writes.

For the "probe" comparison, track **`seconds_first_entries_http`** for first-page fetch time.

User wants to compare:
- `seconds_check_new_data` = first page HTTP time (used in both incremental and full modes)
- `seconds_sync_table` = local sync time</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Here is what was added.

### 1. `pipeline/gf_client.py`
- Introduced **`IncrementalFetchResult`** (`entries` + **`page_timings_seconds`** per `/entries` HTTP call).
- **`fetch_incremental_entries`** now returns that object and records **`perf_counter()`** around each **`fetch_entries_page`** call.

### 2. `pipeline/runner.py` (this is what `run_gf_pipeline.py` drives)
For each form, the run log (`database/pipeline/bowling_bayern/logs/run_{run_id}.json`) now includes:

**`forms[].benchmark`**
- **`seconds_new_data_probe`** — time for the **first** Gravity Forms `/entries` page (same ordering as today: `date_updated` DESC). That is the earliest point you can tell what’s new vs `last_seen_date_updated` on incremental runs.
- **`seconds_all_entry_fetch`** — **sum** of all entry HTTP pages for that form (incremental multi-page or full pagination).
- **`seconds_table_sync`** — **local** work after the last fetch: `rows_from_entries` → sanitize → staging/sanitized CSV → state → **`_run_gf_canonical_legacy`** (merge + `latest.csv` when applicable).
- **`ratio_all_entry_fetch_to_sync`** / **`ratio_probe_to_sync`** — fetch ÷ sync (or `null` if sync time is 0).
- **`metadata`** — `form_id`, `mode`, `site_host`, `page_size`, `verify_ssl`, `python`, `platform`, `pages_fetched`, `legacy_skipped`, and row counts when present, plus a short **`benchmark_note`** explaining the metrics.

**`benchmark_totals`** (top level on the same manifest) sums those seconds across all forms in the run and adds the same ratios and **`forms_processed`**.

### 3. `scripts/run_gf_pipeline.py`
After the main JSON on stdout, a **one-line `[benchmark] ...` summary** is printed to **stderr** so quick runs show totals without opening the log file.

**Note:** Gravity Forms does not expose a separate “lightweight” check endpoint in this code; **“new data” is inferred from the first sorted page**, and pulling all changed rows may take extra pages, hence **probe** vs **all fetch** vs **sync** as three related numbers, with the two ratios comparing fetch totals and first page to sync as you asked conceptually.

The stderr output displays benchmark_totals, which are cumulative times across all forms in the run. The fetch_all_pages_s metric represents the total HTTP time across all forms and pages. The sync_table_s metric continues the sequence. - The user notes that the second run with no updates took around 3 seconds for all forms.
- The metric `sync_table_s=3.99216` refers specifically to local table sync time, not the full run duration.

When running incrementally with no new entries, the API still returns the first page of entries sorted by date_updated DESC, and the client filters for entries newer than `since`. If the first page contains only entries older than or equal to `since`, it may still fetch that page and stop. This means at least one HTTP round-trip per form is unavoidable.

A 69-second fetch time with "no updates" could indicate:
- Multiple forms, each performing a multi-page sweep before encountering old data
- A full sync instead of incremental behavior
- Network latency issues

- The metric `sync_table_s=3.99216` refers specifically to local table sync time, not the full run duration.
- The full run duration includes approximately 69 seconds of Gravity Forms HTTP activity (fetch_all_pages).
- If there are no updates, an incremental run should typically The user asks if ~3 seconds represents the entire second run.

**Answer:** No. The ~3.99s is only the `seconds_table_sync` portion (local CPU/disk). The total wall time includes ~69s of HTTP activity and ~4s of local sync, making the total dominated by the ~69s of remote processing. The probe time is part of the fetch total, so the full runtime is closer to ~73 seconds. Wait  are fetch pages sequential across forms? Yes, the loop processes each form sequentially: fetch then sync. So total time  sum per form (fetch_i + sync_i), not 69 + 4 if overlapping. It's sequential, so roughly **69 + 4 = 73 seconds** wall time, with first page times summed into 33s. If 2 forms, first page each could be ~16s.

Clarifying for the user:

_**Agent (default)**_

No. The **`sync_table_s=3.99216`** line is only the **local** slice of the run: staging/sanitize, merge, writing `latest.csv` / canonical, state — summed over **every form** in that run (`benchmark_totals`).

The **Gravity Forms work** for the same run is the other number: **`fetch_all_pages_s=69.236741`** (again, summed over forms/pages). That is **not** included in the 3.99s.

So for that run, wall time is roughly **~69s HTTP + ~4s local ≈ on the order of 70s+**, not “~3s for everything.” The **`probe_first_page_s=33.416618`** is the **sum of the first `/entries` page** per form (first page is also part of the 69s total fetch, not extra on top).

**Why “no updates” can still show a large `fetch_all_pages`:** incremental still calls the API (often at least one page, sometimes more until it hits entries at or before `last_seen_date_updated`). Slow site, TLS, or several forms will push those seconds up.

**Where to see per form:** each `forms[].benchmark` in the same `run_*.json` has its own `seconds_table_sync` and `seconds_all_entry_fetch` so you can see which form dominates.

_**User**_

this seems like rediculously lousy performance ... so figuring out whether there is new data takes like 95% of the time that actually fetching the data?

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user is confused about benchmark metrics and believes that determining if new data exists consumes 95% of the time, compared to simply fetching the data. The user misunderstands the benchmark metrics. The "probe" is not separate from data fetchingit is the first page of the same `/entries` API call, which includes full JSON entry payloads. The measurement includes:

- **seconds_new_data_probe**: Time for the first HTTP page (full JSON entry data)
- **seconds_all_entry_fetch**: Total time for all HTTP pages including the first page

The "probe" is the initial chunk of the download stream, not a separate operation. The user incorrectly believes that determining new data consumes 95% of the time, thinking the "probe" (first page) is separate from data fetching. In reality, the probe is the initial page of the `/entries` API call, which includes full JSON entry payloads. The actual measurement includes both the probe and all subsequent pages.

- The probe takes 33 seconds, while the remaining pages take approximately 36 seconds (69 - 33).
- This means the probe accounts for about 92% of the total time when compared to the remaining pages.
- The user may have misunderstood the benchmark metrics, thinking the probe is separate from data fetching instead of being part of it.

The confusion arises because:
- The user compares the first page latency to the latency of additional pages.
- They likely interpret this as "probe vs (all_fetch - probe)" rather than recognizing that the probe is included in the total fetch time. First page latency is high due to cold connections, TLS handshakes, and large initial payload. Subsequent pages are faster because of connection reuse and smaller batch sizes.

The user may be comparing the probe (initial page) to sync_table, which creates a 17x differencethis reflects "fetch vs local sync," not "probe vs fetch."

Key clarifications:
- There is no separate "check new data" API.
- The "probe" refers to the same GF `/entries` call that downloads the newest page of entries.
- The probe is not a lightweight existence check but the first page of the full JSON payload.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Here is the important distinction.

### What those numbers actually measure

**`seconds_new_data_probe`** is **not** a separate “is there new data?” call. It is the **wall time of the first full Gravity Forms `/entries` request** — same endpoint, same **full JSON page of entries** (up to `page_size` rows), same work as “fetching data.”

**`seconds_all_entry_fetch`** is the **sum of every `/entries` page** in that run, **including that first page**.

So you are **not** comparing “cheap check” vs “heavy download.” You are comparing **first page** vs **first + second + … + last page**.  

If the first page is ~33s and all pages ~69s, then **later pages add ~36s** — so the first response is a large share of **total HTTP time**, often because of:

- **One slow first response** (TLS, WordPress, DB, cold cache, big payload).
- **Same connection / server** sometimes getting faster on follow-ups — or the opposite, depending on hosting.
- **`urllib`** not reusing connections as well as a `requests.Session()` would, so each call may pay more setup than needed.

So it is **not** that “figuring out new data” is 95% of “actually fetching”; it is that **the first chunk of the same fetch** is a big fraction of **all** fetch time, which is normal when the first round-trip is expensive.

### How this relates to `sync_table_s`

**`sync_table_s`** is **local** work (CSV, merge, `latest.csv`). Comparing **69s network** to **4s disk/CPU** says the bottleneck is **the remote API + payload size**, not your merge step.

### If you want it to feel less “lousy”

Real improvements are things like:

- **Fewer / smaller fields** per entry if GF supports `_field_ids` for a slimmer first payload.
- **Smaller `page_size` for incremental** (trade more round-trips for smaller responses — not always a win).
- **HTTP client with keep-alive** (e.g. `requests` + `Session`) so follow-up pages do not redo as much connection setup.
- **Push instead of poll** (webhooks / server notifies you) so you do not hit `/entries` on every “no change” run.

There is **no** “lightweight GF call” in the current pipeline that only answers “any updates?” without downloading entry bodies; the design is “pull pages until you know you are past `since`.”

If you want, we can add a third metric like **`seconds_entry_fetch_excluding_first_page`** so the log makes the “first page vs rest of fetch” split obvious and avoids this misread.

_**User**_

nah, its fine.
what i meant by "95%":
it seems it takes sth like 30s to figure out that changes occured and then another like 35s to actually get the data. therefor it does not seems plausible to figure it out in the first place and just dump the data no matter what. am i still geting it wrong?

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user seeks clarification regarding their understanding or conceptual framework. They question why discovering changes takes 30 seconds and downloading data takes another 35 seconds, expecting a single "dump" operation instead. The actual process isn't two separate phases but a single incremental flow:

- First HTTP request retrieves a full page of entries (e.g., 100 newest by date_updated). The initial HTTP request takes about 30 seconds, primarily due to network latency, server processing, and JSON parsingthis step is where most of the delay occurs, not during filtering. Filtering changes afterward is a fast O(1) CPU operation once the payload arrives.

Additional pages require further requests, each returning full pages and adding approximately 35 seconds per page or similar time allocation.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

You’re still mixing two different things.

**What actually happens**

1. **The ~30s “first page”** is not a separate “figure out if anything changed” phase. It is **one full HTTP round-trip** where WordPress already sends you a **full page of entry JSON** (same payload you’d need to “just dump” anyway). Almost all of that time is **waiting on the server + network + parsing a big body**, not your code “deciding.”

2. **The ~35s “rest”** is **more of the same**: additional `/entries` pages, each again a full chunk of data. The incremental logic only **stops early** when it sees entries with `date_updated` at or before your `since` watermark (so you might not pull the entire form history).

So it is **not**: “30s to detect changes, then 35s to download.”  
It is: **“~30s for the first downloaded page, ~35s more for further downloaded pages.”** Both are **fetching data**; the first page already **contains** the newest rows. “Figuring out” is cheap string/datetime compares **after** each page arrives.

**“Just dump the data no matter what”**

That would be **full sync every run**: many pages, huge total time, every time. Incremental tries to **stop pulling pages** once older entries appear on the current page — so you **trade** extra logic for **fewer** pages when nothing (or little) is new. The first page is still expensive because the **smallest useful pull** is still “give me the newest N entries” from GF, and that one response is what costs ~30s in your environment.

So: you’re not getting a free 30s “probe” separate from data; you’re seeing **where wall time goes on the first big response vs later big responses**. If that first response is 30s, that’s a **hosting/API/payload** issue, not evidence of a redundant “discovery” step you could delete and keep the same behavior.

k, thanks for clarification. I think I need to talk to the data owner so we get a sort of meta form that tells us the timestamps of the forms of interest. also I will discuss the possbilities for webhooks on incoming data 

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user plans to discuss the matter with the data owner.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

That sounds like a solid direction.

A **small “meta” form** (or even a single custom REST field / options row) that only exposes **last-updated per source form** lets you do a **cheap poll** first and skip the heavy `/entries` pull when nothing moved—exactly the missing piece in the current GF-only flow.

**Webhooks** are the other natural win: Gravity Forms can fire on entry create/update so your side **pushes** work instead of **pulling** on a timer; you still want **idempotency** and maybe a periodic full reconcile in case a webhook is missed.

Good luck with the data owner conversation. If you later want help sketching the meta contract or a minimal webhook receiver in this repo, say what stack you prefer for the receiver (same Flask app vs separate worker).

            "text": "            color: var(--theme-text-on-secondary);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 81,
            "text": "        }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 82
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 83,
            "text": "        .btn-primary:hover {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 84,
            "text": "            background-color: var(--theme-primary);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 85,
            "text": "            border-color: var(--theme-primary);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 86,
            "text": "            color: var(--theme-text-on-primary);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 87,
            "text": "        }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 88
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 89,
            "text": "        /* Outline Button Variants */"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 90,
            "text": "        .btn-outline-primary {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 91,
            "text": "            color: var(--theme-secondary);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 92,
            "text": "            border-color: var(--theme-secondary);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 93,
            "text": "        }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 94
          },

            "text": "        .btn-outline-primary:hover {"
          },

        "range": {
          "endPosition": {
            "column": 12,

    },
    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "lineNumber": 1,
            "text": "{% extends \"base.html\" %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 2
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 3,
            "text": "{% block title %}Home{% endblock %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 4
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 5,
            "text": "{% block content %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 6,
            "text": "\u003c!--h1\u003eBowling Dashboard\u003c/h1--\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 7,
            "text": "\u003cdiv class=\"row mt-4\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 8,
            "text": "    \u003cdiv class=\"col-md-6\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 9,
            "text": "        \u003cdiv class=\"card mb-4\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 10,
            "text": "            \u003cdiv class=\"card-header\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 11,
            "text": "                Info"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 12,
            "text": "            \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 13,
            "text": "            \u003cdiv class=\"card-body\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 14,
            "text": "                \u003c!-- We'll add stats here later --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 15,
            "text": "                \u003cp\u003eDiese Seite ist ein Proof of Concept zur Darstellung von Ergebnissen und Statistiken rund um Bowling Ligen, Tuniere und Pokalwettbewerbe.\u003c/p\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 16,
            "text": "                \u003cp\u003eDie Auswertungen sind oben über die Navigation zu \"League Stats\" und \"Player Stats\" zu finden, ich hab auch ein paar Beispiele verlinkt.\u003c/p\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 17,
            "text": "                \u003cp\u003eHauptaugenmerk liegt derzeit auf den Ligastatistiken. Die meisten Daten sind fiktiv, jedoch ist die Seite so aufgabeut, dass reale Daten einfach integriert werden könnten.\u003c/p\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 18,
            "text": "                \u003cp\u003eIhr könnte auf den Seiten meist die Ebene der angezeigten Infos verändern, in dem ihr die Buttons für z.B. die Saison auswählt. Einfach ein wenig herumprobieren.\u003c/p\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 19,
            "text": "                "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 20,
            "text": "                \u003cp\u003eBitte kommt mit Feedback und Vorschlägen gerne per Whatsapp oder im Traning auf mich zu.\u003c/p\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 21,
            "text": "                \u003cp\u003e  Cheers, Chris\u003c/p\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 22,
            "text": "            \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 23,
            "text": "        \u003c/div\u003e       "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 24,
            "text": "    \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 25
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 26
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 27,
            "text": "    \u003c!-- New Examples Card --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 28,
            "text": "    \u003cdiv class=\"col-md-6 mt-4\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 29,
            "text": "        \u003cdiv class=\"card\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 30,
            "text": "            \u003cdiv class=\"card-header\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 31,
            "text": "                Beispiele"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 32,
            "text": "            \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 33,
            "text": "            \u003cdiv class=\"card-body\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 34,
            "text": "                \u003cul class=\"list-group\" id=\"exampleQueries\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 35,
            "text": "                    \u003c!-- Will be populated via JavaScript --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 36,
            "text": "                \u003c/ul\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 37,
            "text": "            \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 38,
            "text": "        \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 39,
            "text": "    \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 40,
            "text": "   "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 41,
            "text": "    \u003cdiv class=\"col-md-6\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 42,
            "text": "        "
          }
        ],
        "range": {
          "endPosition": {
            "column": 8,
            "line": 41
          },
          "startPosition": {}
        },
        "relativeWorkspacePath": ".\\league_analyzer_v1\\app\\templates\\index.html",
        "signatures": {}
      },
      "score": 0.1737287938594818
    },
    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "lineNumber": 662,
            "text": "columnDefinition.frozen = groupFrozenDirection;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 663,
            "text": "                }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 664
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 665,
            "text": "                // Column widths: simple approach based on compact flag"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 666,
            "text": "                let columnWidth = parseColumnWidth(column.width) || 100;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 667,
            "text": "                "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 668,
            "text": "                if (isCompactLayout) {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 669,
            "text": "                    // Compact mode: fit to content, don't grow beyond content needs"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 670,
            "text": "                    columnDefinition.minWidth = columnWidth;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 671,
            "text": "                    columnDefinition.widthGrow = 0;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 672,
            "text": "                } else {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 673,
            "text": "                    // Non-compact mode: expand columns proportionally based on widthGrow"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 674,
            "text": "                    columnDefinition.minWidth = columnWidth;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 675,
            "text": "                    const isTextColumn = !isNumberColumn \u0026\u0026 !isStringNumber;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 676,
            "text": "                    const isTeamColumn = field \u0026\u0026 (field.includes('team') || field.includes('player') || field.includes('name'));"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 677,
            "text": "                    columnDefinition.widthGrow = isTeamColumn ? 3 : (isTextColumn ? 2 : 1);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 678,
            "text": "                }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 679
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 680,
            "text": "                tabulatorColumns.push(columnDefinition);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 681,
            "text": "            });"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 682,
            "text": "        });"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 683,
            "text": "    } else {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 684,
            "text": "        // Normal structure with groups"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 685,
            "text": "        tabulatorColumns = (data.columns || []).map((group, groupIndex) =\u003e {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 686,
            "text": "        if (!Array.isArray(group.columns)) {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 687,
            "text": "            return null;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 688,
            "text": "        }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 689
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 690,
            "text": "        const groupFrozenDirection = group.frozen === 'left' || group.frozen === 'right' ? group.frozen : null;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 691,
            "text": "        "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 692,
            "text": "        // Use backend flag for highlighting"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 693,
            "text": "        const isHighlighted = group.highlighted === true;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 694
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 695,
            "text": "        // Build cssClass: combine striped class (already added by assignGroupStripeCss) with highlight class"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 696,
            "text": "        let groupCssClass = group.cssClass || '';"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 697,
            "text": "        if (isHighlighted) {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 698,
            "text": "            groupCssClass = (groupCssClass + ' tab-group-highlighted').trim();"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 699,
            "text": "        }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 700
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 701,
            "text": "        const groupDefinition = {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 702,
            "text": "            title: group.title || '',"
          }
        ],
        "range": {
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 702
          },
          "startPosition": {
            "column": 20,
            "line": 661
          }
        },

            "text": "    const tabulatorOptions = {"

          },

            "lineNumber": 4,
            "text": "\u003cdiv class=\"row\"\u003e"

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "isSignature": true,
            "lineNumber": 35,
            "text": "function generateLeagueStandingsCardHTML(options) {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 56
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 57,
            "text": "    "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 58,
            "text": "    return &#96;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 59,
            "text": "        \u003cdiv class=\"row\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 60,
            "text": "            \u003cdiv class=\"col-md-8\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 61,
            "text": "                \u003ch6\u003e${typeof t === 'function' ? t('league_standings', 'League Standings') : 'League Standings'}\u003c/h6\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 62,
            "text": "                \u003cdiv id=\"${safeTableId}\"\u003e\u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 63,
            "text": "            \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 64,
            "text": "            \u003cdiv class=\"col-md-4\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 65,
            "text": "                \u003cdiv class=\"card\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 66,
            "text": "                    \u003cdiv class=\"card-header\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 67,
            "text": "                        \u003ch6\u003e${typeof t === 'function' ? t('honor_scores', 'Honor Scores') : 'Honor Scores'}\u003c/h6\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 68,
            "text": "                    \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 69,
            "text": "                    \u003cdiv class=\"card-body\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 70,
            "text": "                        \u003c!-- Individual Scores --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 71,
            "text": "                        \u003cdiv class=\"mb-3\" id=\"${individualScoresSectionId}\" style=\"display: none;\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 72,
            "text": "                            \u003ch6\u003e${typeof t === 'function' ? t('top_individual_scores', 'Top Individual Scores') : 'Top Individual Scores'}\u003c/h6\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 73,
            "text": "                            \u003cdiv id=\"${individualScoresId}\"\u003e\u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 74,
            "text": "                        \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 75,
            "text": "                        \u003c!-- Team Scores --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 76,
            "text": "                        \u003cdiv class=\"mb-3\" id=\"${teamScoresSectionId}\" style=\"display: none;\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 77,

      "codeBlock": {
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "lineNumber": 1,
            "text": "{% extends \"base.html\" %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 2
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 3,
            "text": "{% block title %}API Test Page{% endblock %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 4
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 5,
            "text": "{% block content %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 6,
            "text": "{% include \"components/tables.html\" %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 7,
            "text": "{% include \"components/charts.html\" %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 8
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 9,
            "text": "\u003cdiv class=\"container-fluid\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 10,
            "text": "    \u003ch1 class=\"mb-4\"\u003eAPI Test Page\u003c/h1\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 11,
            "text": "    "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 12,
            "text": "    \u003c!-- Selection Area --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 13,
            "text": "    \u003cdiv class=\"card mb-4\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 14,
            "text": "        \u003cdiv class=\"card-header\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 15,
            "text": "            \u003ch4\u003eSelection Controls\u003c/h4\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 16,
            "text": "        \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 17,
            "text": "        \u003cdiv class=\"card-body\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 18,
            "text": "            \u003cdiv class=\"row mb-3\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 19,
            "text": "                \u003cdiv class=\"col-md-4\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 20,
            "text": "                    \u003ch5\u003eSeason\u003c/h5\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 21,
            "text": "                    \u003cdiv id=\"seasonSelector\" class=\"btn-group-horizontal w-100\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 22,
            "text": "                        \u003c!-- Seasons will be inserted here --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 23,
            "text": "                    \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 24,
            "text": "                \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 25,
            "text": "                \u003cdiv class=\"col-md-4\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 26,
            "text": "                    \u003ch5\u003eLeague\u003c/h5\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 27,
            "text": "                    \u003cdiv id=\"leagueSelector\" class=\"btn-group-horizontal w-100\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 28,
            "text": "                        \u003c!-- Leagues will be inserted here --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 29,
            "text": "                    \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 30,
            "text": "                \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 31,
            "text": "                \u003cdiv class=\"col-md-4\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 32,
            "text": "                    \u003ch5\u003eWeek\u003c/h5\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 33,
            "text": "                    \u003cdiv id=\"weekSelector\" class=\"btn-group-horizontal w-100\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 34,
            "text": "                        \u003c!-- Weeks will be inserted here --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 35,
            "text": "                    \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 36,
            "text": "                \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 37,
            "text": "            \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 38,
            "text": "            \u003cdiv class=\"row\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 39,
            "text": "                \u003cdiv class=\"col-md-12\"\u003e"
          },
          {

            "text": "                    \u003ch5\u003eTeam\u003c/h5\u003e"

          {

          },
          {

          },

          },

          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 188,
            "text": "\u003c!-- Custom Styles for API Test Page --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 189,
            "text": "\u003cstyle\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 190,
            "text": ".json-key { color: #d73a49; }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 191,
            "text": ".json-string { color: #032f62; }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 192,
            "text": ".json-number { color: #005cc5; }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 193,
            "text": ".json-boolean { color: #e36209; }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 194,
            "text": ".json-null { color: #6f42c1; }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 195
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 196,
            "text": ".route-item {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 197,
            "text": "    transition: all 0.2s ease;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 198,
            "text": "}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 199
          },
          {

            "text": ".route-item:hover {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 201,
            "text": "    background-color: #f8f9fa;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 202,
            "text": "    border-color: #6f42c1 !important;"
          },
          {

            "text": "}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 204
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 205,
            "text": ".route-endpoint {"
          },
          {

            "text": "    font-family: 'SF Mono', Monaco, 'Cascadia Code', 'Roboto Mono', Consolas, 'Courier New', monospace;"

          {

          },

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "lineNumber": 1,
            "text": "\u003c!DOCTYPE html\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 2,
            "text": "\u003chtml lang=\"en\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 3,
            "text": "\u003chead\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 4,
            "text": "    \u003cmeta charset=\"UTF-8\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 5,
            "text": "    \u003cmeta name=\"viewport\" content=\"width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 6,
            "text": "    \u003ctitle\u003eVue.js Table Test - League Analyzer\u003c/title\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 7,
            "text": "    "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 8,
            "text": "    \u003c!-- Bootstrap CSS (keep existing styling) --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 9,
            "text": "    \u003clink href=\"https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.0/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css\" rel=\"stylesheet\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 10,
            "text": "    "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 11,
            "text": "    \u003cstyle\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 12,
            "text": "        .table-container {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 13,
            "text": "            margin: 20px 0;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 14,
            "text": "        }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 15,
            "text": "        .loading {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 16,
            "text": "            text-align: center;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 17,
            "text": "            padding: 20px;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 18,
            "text": "        }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 19,
            "text": "    \u003c/style\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 20,
            "text": "\u003c/head\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 21,
            "text": "\u003cbody\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 22,
            "text": "    \u003cdiv id=\"app\" class=\"container mt-4\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 23,
            "text": "        \u003ch1\u003eVue.js Table Test\u003c/h1\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 24,
            "text": "        "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 25,
            "text": "        \u003cdiv class=\"card\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 26,
            "text": "            \u003cdiv class=\"card-header\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 27,
            "text": "                \u003ch3\u003eLeague Standings Table\u003c/h3\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 28,
            "text": "            \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 29,
            "text": "            \u003cdiv class=\"card-body\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 30,
            "text": "                {% raw %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 31,
            "text": "                \u003c!-- Loading state --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 32,
            "text": "                \u003cdiv v-if=\"loading\" class=\"loading\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 33,
            "text": "                    \u003cdiv class=\"spinner-border\" role=\"status\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 34,
            "text": "                        \u003cspan class=\"visually-hidden\"\u003eLoading...\u003c/span\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 35,
            "text": "                    \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 36,
            "text": "                \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 37,
            "text": "                "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 38,
            "text": "                \u003c!-- Error state --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 39,
            "text": "                \u003cdiv v-if=\"error\" class=\"alert alert-danger\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 40,
            "text": "                    {{ error }}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 41,
            "text": "                \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 42,
            "text": "                {% endraw %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 43,
            "text": "                "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 44,
            "text": "                {% raw %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 45,
            "text": "                \u003c!-- Table component --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 46,
            "text": "                \u003cdata-table "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 47,
            "text": "                    v-if=\"!loading \u0026\u0026 !error \u0026\u0026 tableData\""
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 48,
            "text": "                    :data=\"tableData.data\""
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 49,
            "text": "                    :columns=\"tableData.columns\""
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 50,
            "text": "                    :title=\"tableData.title\""
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 51,
            "text": "                \u003e\u003c/data-table\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 52,
            "text": "                "
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 53,
            "text": "                \u003c!-- No data state --\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 54,
            "text": "                \u003cdiv v-if=\"!loading \u0026\u0026 !error \u0026\u0026 !tableData\" class=\"alert alert-info\"\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 55,
            "text": "                    No data available"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 56,
            "text": "                \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 57,
            "text": "                {% endraw %}"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 58,
            "text": "            \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 59,
            "text": "        \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 60,
            "text": "    \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 61
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 62,
            "text": "    \u003c!-- Vue.js 3 from CDN --\u003e"
          }
        ],
        "range": {
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 62
          },
          "startPosition": {}

          },

          },

          {

            "text": "                        \u003cdiv class=\"table-responsive\"\u003e"
          },

            "text": "                    \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 67,
            "text": "                    \u003cdiv id=\"standings-error\" class=\"alert alert-danger\" style=\"display: none;\"\u003e\u003c/div\u003e"
          },
