The user demonstrated that entry 28729 can be accessed directly using the endpoint GET /api/gf/entries/28729, suggesting a custom API route rather than the standard GF REST API endpoint wp-json/gf/v2/entries/{id}. The entry with ID 28729 is accessible via direct API call but missing from paginated listings, indicating a pagination or listing bug rather than deletion. This suggests two potential pipeline fixes:

- Add a repair pass to fetch missing entries individually by ID
- Modify fetch strategy to use alternative sorting or page sizes

This discrepancy implies the system's batch endpoint has limitations while individual entry retrieval functions correctly. The user's findings confirm that paged listings omit entries which are retrievable via direct GET requests, reinforcing earlier observations of 26 missing IDs across all pages. This inconsistency indicates a flaw in the batch listing mechanism rather than an issue with individual entry access.

Potential mitigations include:
- Documenting the incomplete nature of paged listings for GF
- Recommending full exports or individual fetches as alternatives
- Implementing a repair pass to retrieve missing entries by ID
- Adjusting fetch strategies to avoid pagination issues</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

This is useful evidence: it **does not** contradict what we saw in `incoming/...__page_*.json`.

## What it shows

- **Single-entry GET** for `28729` returns **200** and a normal **active** payload (`status: "active"`, sensible fields, `date_updated` set).
- For run `20260412T200955Z`, that same id appears on **no** paged **`/entries`** response file we saved.

So the problem is **not** “row deleted / invisible to the API”. It is **“paged list endpoint does not include this id (and 25 others), while by-id read still does.”** That is exactly the kind of failure you get when **ordering + paging is unstable or incomplete** for rows that share the same `date_updated` second (your missing set clustered on `2026-02-15 09:48:21/25/28/32`).

Your URL shape (`/api/gf/entries/28729`) may be a proxy in front of Gravity Forms’ usual `GET /wp-json/gf/v2/entries/{id}`; the important part is **by-id vs list**.

## Implications for the pipeline

- **Merging** is not where those rows are lost; they are already absent from **per-page list** JSON.
- **Tuning `page_size`** might change which rows overlap or drop (worth an A/B), but it is **empirical** only.
- A **reliable** fix is to stop relying on “one sorted paged pass” as the complete set, e.g.:
  - **Reconciliation**: after the paged pull, compute missing ids (vs export or vs `max(id)` / known id list) and **`GET entries/{id}`** for each gap; or  
  - **Different list query** if the server supports a stable sort (e.g. by `id` ascending) or a filter that avoids the bad bucket; or  
  - **Official export** as the authority for “which ids exist”, then **fill** from the API by id.

If you want this in code next, the natural addition is a small `GfClient.fetch_entry(entry_id)` (or `GET .../entries/{id}`) plus an optional **“repair missing ids”** step that takes a list of ids (from CSV diff) and merges them into staging before canonical merge. I can wire that into `run_gf_pipeline` / `run_ingest` when you say you want it.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Gravity Forms does not provide a direct option to sort entries by entry ID when fetching via API. The default sorting is typically based on date_updated, which can cause issues with pagination when multiple entries have identical timestamps. Users may need to implement custom sorting logic after retrieving the entries or explore third-party solutions that offer enhanced sorting capabilities.</details></think>

```json
{
  "markdown": "Searching and Getting Entries with the REST API v2 - Gravity Forms Documentation\n\n# Searching and Getting Entries with the REST API v2\n\n- [Knowledge Base](https://docs.gravityforms.com/category/knowledge-base/)\n- Introduction\n- Authentication\n- Getting a Specific Entry\n- Method\n- Path\n- Required Properties\n- Optional Properties\n- Response\n- Success\n- Failure\n- Usage Examples\n- cURL Request\n- Response\n- cURL Request\n- Response\n- Searching Entries\n- Method\n- Path\n- Required Properties\n- Optional Properties\n- Search Arguments\n- Field Filters\n- Sorting Arguments\n- Paging Arguments\n- Response [json]\n- Usage Examples\n- Retrieve specific entry\n- Retrieve specific fields\n- Include specific forms\n- Include specific entries\n- Use paging\n- Use sorting\n- Search entries\n\n## Introduction\n\nThe&#96;/entries&#96;,&#96;/forms/[FORM_ID]/entries&#96;, and&#96;/entries/[ENTRY_ID]&#96; endpoints are used to search and get entries.\n\n## Authentication\n\nSee [REST API v2 Authentication](https://docs.gravityforms.com/rest-api-v2-authentication/).\n\nThe [capability](https://docs.gravityforms.com/role-management-guide/)&#96;gravityforms_view_entries&#96; is required to use&#96;GET&#96; requests at these endpoints.This can be filtered using [gform_rest_api_capability_get_entries](https://docs.gravityforms.com/gform_rest_api_capability_get_entries/).\n\n## Getting a Specific Entry\n\nThe&#96;/entries/[ENTRY_ID]&#96; endpoint is used to get an entry by it’s ID.\n\n### Method\n\nThis endpoint accepts&#96;GET&#96; requests in order to retrieve an entry.\n\n### Path\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n/gf/v2/entries/1\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Required Properties\n\nThere are no required properties.\n\n### Optional Properties\n\nThe request URL can also include the following parameters.\n\n| Key | Type | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| _field_ids | string | A comma separated list of fields to include in the response. When including this property, only the explicitly requested entry properties will be included in the response. |\n| _labels | integer | Default is&#96;0&#96;. Indicates whether to enable the inclusion of field labels in the results. |\n\n### Response\n\n#### Success\n\nA successful response will contain a JSON object with the entry details.\n\n| Key | Type | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| [entry_property] | mixed | Entry properties of the [Entry Object](https://docs.gravityforms.com/entry-object/). |\n| [input_id] | string | Submission values of each form field input for the entry. |\n| _labels | JSON object | Only present when&#96;_labels&#96; is&#96;1&#96; in request.Contains field input and labels pairing for the form. |\n\n#### Failure\n\nA failed response will provide a JSON string of the error code and message.\n\n| Key | Type | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| code | string | Error code.&#96;gf_entry_invalid_id&#96; |\n| message | string | Human-readable error message. |\n| data[status] | integer | [HTTP response status code](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Status) |\n\n### Usage Examples\n\n#### cURL Request\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl \"https://rocket.test/wp-json/gf/v2/entries/159?_labels=1\" \\\n     -u 'ck_5f86565df60696c43af25f9194e106800770b8e9:cs_be0190310fefc061c564168670d0a96d68873c29'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Response\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n{\n  \"id\": \"159\",\n  \"form_id\": \"30\",\n  \"post_id\": null,\n  \"date_created\": \"2023-09-01 19:30:44\",\n  \"date_updated\": \"2023-09-01 19:30:44\",\n  \"is_starred\": \"0\",\n  \"is_read\": \"1\",\n  \"ip\": \"50.104.76.242\",\n  \"source_url\": \"https:\\/\\/rocket.twhl.dev\\/wp-json\\/gf\\/v2\\/entries\",\n  \"user_agent\": \"API\",\n  \"currency\": \"USD\",\n  \"payment_status\": null,\n  \"payment_date\": null,\n  \"payment_amount\": null,\n  \"payment_method\": \"\",\n  \"transaction_id\": null,\n  \"is_fulfilled\": null,\n  \"created_by\": \"4\",\n  \"transaction_type\": null,\n  \"status\": \"active\",\n  \"1.3\": \"Neil\",\n  \"1.6\": \"Armstrong\",\n  \"3\": \"[email protected]\",\n  \"4\": \"To the moon!\",\n  \"1.2\": \"\",\n  \"1.4\": \"\",\n  \"1.8\": \"\",\n  \"_labels\": {\n    \"1\": {\n      \"1\": \"Name\",\n      \"1.2\": \"Prefix\",\n      \"1.3\": \"First\",\n      \"1.4\": \"Middle\",\n      \"1.6\": \"Last\",\n      \"1.8\": \"Suffix\"\n    },\n    \"3\": \"Email\",\n    \"4\": \"Message\"\n  }\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### cURL Request\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl \"https://rocket.test/wp-json/gf/v2/entries/159?_field_ids=1.6,4\" \\\n     -u 'ck_5f86565df60696c43af25f9194e106800770b8e9:cs_be0190310fefc061c564168670d0a96d68873c29'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Response\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n{\n  \"date_created\": \"2023-09-01 19:30:44\",\n  \"1.6\": \"Armstrong\",\n  \"4\": \"To the moon!\"\n}\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Searching Entries\n\nThe&#96;/entries&#96; endpoint is used to get all entries or all entries matching specific search parameters\n\n### Method\n\nThese endpoints accept&#96;GET&#96; requests in order to retrieve entries.\n\n### Path\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n/gf/v2/entries\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nor\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n/gf/v2/forms/[FORM_ID]/entries\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Required Properties\n\nThere are no required properties.\n\n### Optional Properties\n\nThe request URL can also include the following parameters.\n\n| Key | Type | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| _field_ids | string | A comma separated list of fields to include in the response. When including this property, only the explicitly requested entry properties will be included in the response. |\n| _labels | integer | Default is&#96;0&#96;. Indicates whether to enable the inclusion of field labels in the results. |\n| form_ids | array | An array of forms for which entries should be included.Only available when using the endpoint&#96;/gf/v2/entries&#96;. |\n| include | array | An array of entries to include in the response. If an included entry id is not found in the database, no error is thrown. |\n| search | array | Optional.An associative array containing the search arguments.It is expected that&#96;search&#96; will be provided as JSON. |\n| sorting | array | Optional.An associative array containing the sorting arguments. |\n| paging | null|array | Optional.An associative array containing the paging arguments.IMPORTANT TIP: use paging to limit the number of entries otherwise you may find the database times out. |\n\n#### Search Arguments\n\nAn associative array supporting the following properties. Must be provided in request as JSON.\n\n| Prop | Type | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| status | string | Default is active.The status of the entry. Possible values are active, spam, trash. |\n| field_filters | array | See Field Filters |\n\n#### Field Filters\n\n| Prop | Type | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| [array](https://docs.gravityforms.com/array) | One or more associative arrays. See Field Filter. |\n| mode | [string](https://docs.gravityforms.com/string) | Optional.Defaults to&#96;all&#96;.Possible values:&#96;all&#96; or&#96;any&#96;.Determines if the found entries have to match all the field filters or any. |\n\n##### Field Filter\n\nAn associative array supporting the following properties.\n\n| Prop | Type | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| key | [string](https://docs.gravityforms.com/string) | The field ID, entry property, or entry meta key. |\n| value | [string](https://docs.gravityforms.com/string)| [integer](https://docs.gravityforms.com/integer)| [float](https://docs.gravityforms.com/float)| [array](https://docs.gravityforms.com/array) | The value to find for the specified&#96;key&#96;. |\n| operator | [string](https://docs.gravityforms.com/string) | Optional.Defaults to&#96;=&#96;.Possible values:&#96;=&#96;,&#96;IS&#96;,&#96;CONTAINS&#96;,&#96;IS NOT&#96;,&#96;ISNOT&#96;,&#96;\u003c\u003e&#96;,&#96;LIKE&#96;,&#96;NOT IN&#96;,&#96;NOTIN&#96;, or&#96;IN&#96;.Lowercase is also supported. |\n| is_numeric | [boolean](https://docs.gravityforms.com/boolean) | Optional.Indicates if the values of the specified&#96;key&#96; are numeric. |\n\n#### Sorting Arguments\n\nAn associative array supporting the following properties.\n\n| Prop | Type | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| key | string|integer | Default is entry id. The database field by which to sort. See the database structure for the [wp_gf_entry](https://docs.gravityforms.com/database-storage-structure-reference/#wp-gf-entry) table for fields that may be used. |\n| direction | string | Default is DESC.The direction of sorting. Either ASC, DESC, or RAND (random order). |\n| is_numeric | boolean | Default is false when&#96;sorting[key]&#96; is specified.Indicates if the key is numeric. |\n\n#### Paging Arguments\n\nAn associative array supporting the following properties.\n\n| Prop | Type | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| page_size | integer | The number of results per page. |\n| current_page | integer | The current page from which to pull details. |\n| offset | integer | The record number on which to start retrieving data. It is zero-based. If the&#96;current_page&#96; property is specified, then&#96;offset&#96; is not used. |\n\n### Response [json]\n\nA successful response will contain a JSON object with matching entries.\n\n| Key | Type | Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| total_count | integer | A count of all matching entries. |\n| entries | array | An array of matching entry objects. |\n\n### Usage Examples\n\n#### Retrieve specific entry\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries/112' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Retrieve specific fields\n\nThis example shows how to retrieve values of field IDs 1, 6.1, 6.2, 6.3 and date_created for entries on Form ID 112 while including field labels.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/forms/112/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode '_field_ids=1,6.1,6.2,6.3,date_created' \\\n--data-urlencode '_labels=1'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Include specific forms\n\nThis example shows how to get all entries from Form ID 1 and Form ID 30.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'form_ids[]=1' \\\n--data-urlencode 'form_ids[]=30'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Include specific entries\n\nThis example shows how to get multiple specific entries by entry ID using the&#96;include&#96; property.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'include[]=12' \\\n--data-urlencode 'include[]=45' \\\n--data-urlencode 'include[]=51'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Use paging\n\nThis example shows how to retrieve 20 results per page.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'paging[page_size]=20'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis example shows how to retrieve 20 results per page.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'paging[page_size]=20'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis example shows how to retrieve 20 results per page starting with the 16th row (offset starts at 0)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'paging[page_size]=20' \\\n--data-urlencode 'paging[offset]=15'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis example shows how to retrieve 5 results per page starting with the second page\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'paging[page_size]=5' \\\n--data-urlencode 'paging[current_page]=2'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Use sorting\n\nThis example shows how to sort results ASCending by&#96;id&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'sorting[key]=id' \\\n--data-urlencode 'sorting[direction]=ASC' \\\n--data-urlencode 'sorting[is_numeric]=true'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis example shows how to sort results DESCending by&#96;date_created&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'sorting[key]=date_created' \\\n--data-urlencode 'sorting[direction]=DESC'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis example shows how to sort results randomly when retrieving all enries for Form ID 19\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/forms/19/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'sorting[direction]=RAND'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis example shows how to sort results by Field ID 2\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'sorting[key]=2'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Search entries\n\nThis example shows how to retrieve entries where Field ID 2 contains the text test.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'search={\"field_filters\":[{\"key\":2,\"value\":\"test\",\"operator\":\"contains\"}]}'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis example shows how to retrieve entries where Field ID 2 and Field ID 1.3 contains the text squiffy. Note that since search mode is not specified&#96;all&#96; is used for the&#96;mode&#96;.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'search={\"field_filters\":[{\"key\":2,\"value\":\"squiffy\",\"operator\":\"contains\"},{\"key\":1.3,\"value\":\"squiffy\",\"operator\":\"contains\"}]}'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis example performs the same search as above, but sets&#96;mode&#96; to match against&#96;any&#96; of the provided field filters.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'search={\"field_filters\":[\"mode\":\"any\",\"0\":{\"key\":2,\"value\":\"squiffy\",\"operator\":\"contains\"},\"1\":{\"key\":1.3,\"value\":\"squiffy\",\"operator\":\"contains\"}]}'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis example retrieve entries created on a specific day (i.e. September 10, 2019) by filtering on the&#96;date_created&#96; property of the entry.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\ncurl --location --request GET -G 'https://example.com/wp-json/gf/v2/entries' \\\n\n--user 'alisha:XnVO ccCg lt3X W3Wa AlQQ mf0R' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data-urlencode 'search={\"field_filters\":[{\"key\":\"date_created\",\"value\":\"09/10/2019\",\"operator\":\"is\"}]}'\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;",
  "url": "https://docs.gravityforms.com/searching-and-getting-entries-with-the-rest-api-v2/"
}
```
User decision: **accepted**

Status: **completed**


</details>
</tool-use>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The Gravity Forms documentation is very clear.

- It provides detailed guidance on form creation and customization.
- It includes comprehensive examples and best practices.
- It offers troubleshooting tips for common issues.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Yes. Gravity Forms’ **REST API v2** supports a **`sorting`** object on `GET /wp-json/gf/v2/entries` and `GET /wp-json/gf/v2/forms/{id}/entries`.

From the official doc ([Searching and Getting Entries with the REST API v2](https://docs.gravityforms.com/searching-and-getting-entries-with-the-rest-api-v2/)):

- **`sorting[key]`** — “The database field by which to sort,” with reference to the **`wp_gf_entry`** table (so **`id`**, **`date_created`**, **`date_updated`**, etc. are valid, not only form fields).
- **`sorting[direction]`** — `ASC`, `DESC`, or `RAND`.
- **`sorting[is_numeric]`** — relevant when sorting by **`id`**.

They even give an example for **stable id order**:

```http
sorting[key]=id
sorting[direction]=ASC
sorting[is_numeric]=true
```

So you do **not** need CSV exports to get a deterministic order: you can page by **`id` ASC** (or **`id` DESC**) and avoid the “hundreds of rows share the same `date_updated` second” problem that makes **`date_updated` DESC** paging fragile.

**Caveat in your codebase:** `GfClient.fetch_entries_page` currently always sends `sorting[key]=date_updated` and `sorting[direction]=DESC` when `sort_desc_by_updated=True` (the default). That is a **pipeline choice**, not a GF limitation. Switching full sync (or all fetches) to **`id` + `ASC` + `is_numeric=true`** would be the natural experiment—and it’s officially supported.

**Optional GF feature:** the doc also mentions an **`include[]`** list to pull specific entry IDs in one request; that still needs you to know ids, so it’s less helpful for “100+ forms” unless combined with another strategy.

I can add a `--sort-key` / `--sort-direction` (or default to `id` ASC for full sync) in `run_gf_pipeline` + `GfClient` if you want that wired in next.

---

_**User**_

add sort by id as default, add parameter to go back to sort by timestamp just for the sake of it

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user wants to change the default sorting in the GF client to sort by ID, using stable paging with ID ASC and numeric sorting enabled. 2. Add a parameter to optionally sort by timestamp (date_updated) as before.

I need to modify:
- `gf_client.py` - `fetch_entries_page` and possibly `fetch_incremental_entries` - the incremental flow uses date_updated comparison for watermarking, so sorting by id might still work for paging but the incremental logic iterates entries sorted DESC by date_updated to find cutoff...

Plan:
- GfConfig in config.py might need sort_key, sort_direction, sort_is_numeric - or pass through run_ingest
- GfClient. fetch_entries_page should accept a `sort` parameter with values "id" or "date_updated", defaulting to "id" for stable paging. A `use_date_updated_sort` boolean parameter may also be added for CLI backward compatibility. When sorting by ID, the API should use `sorting[key]=id`, `sorting[direction]=ASC`, and `sorting[is_numeric]=true`. For incremental updates, the system must sort by date_updated in descending order to properly identify the watermark and stop at the correct point. For full synchronization, sorting by ID in ascending order is preferred for stable paging. This ensures that incremental logic works correctly while maintaining stable pagination during full syncs.

- Full sync: sort by ID ASC (natural for paging from start)
- Incremental sync: sort by date_updated DESC (required for proper watermark detection)</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

_**Agent (default)**_

Here is what was implemented.

### Default: sort by entry `id`
- **`GfConfig.entries_sort`** defaults to **`"id"`**.
- **`GfClient.fetch_entries_page`** sends Gravity Forms’ stable sort:  
  `sorting[key]=id`, `sorting[direction]=ASC`, `sorting[is_numeric]=true`  
  (per [GF REST sorting docs](https://docs.gravityforms.com/searching-and-getting-entries-with-the-rest-api-v2/)).

### Opt-in: sort by timestamp (legacy)
- **`--entries-sort date_updated`** uses the old behavior:  
  `sorting[key]=date_updated`, `sorting[direction]=DESC`.
- **`--entries-sort id`** is the default (explicit if you want).

### Incremental unchanged
- **`fetch_incremental_entries`** still calls **`fetch_entries_page(..., entries_sort="date_updated")`**, so the watermark walk stays newest-first by `date_updated`.

### Other wiring
- **`GfConfig.from_env()`** reads **`GF_ENTRIES_SORT`** (`id` or `date_updated`; invalid values fall back to `id`).
- Run manifest / benchmark metadata includes **`entries_sort`**; top-level run JSON includes **`entries_sort`**.

### Files touched
- `league_analyzer_v1/pipeline/config.py` — `entries_sort` field + env in `from_env`
- `league_analyzer_v1/pipeline/gf_client.py` — sort branches; incremental forces `date_updated`
- `league_analyzer_v1/pipeline/runner.py` — manifest note + `entries_sort` in result metadata
- `league_analyzer_v1/scripts/run_gf_pipeline.py` — **`--entries-sort`**

---

[Same record shows up multiple times in ordered paginated query](https://dba.stackexchange.com/questions/123737/same-record-shows-up-multiple-times-in-ordered-paginated-query)\n3. [MySQL Bugs: #69732: LIMIT clause results in duplicate data across pages](https://bugs.mysql.com/bug.php?id=69732)\n4. [Mysql Paging With multiple ORDER BY](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/30165596/mysql-paging-with-multiple-order-by)\n5. [MySQL Bugs: #116366: Pagination order by a datetime column with same value display duplicate rows](https://bugs.mysql.com/bug.php?id=116366)\n\n# MySQL ORDER BY with Duplicate Values and Pagination Issues\n\n## The Core Problem\n\nWhen using &#96;ORDER BY&#96; with duplicate values combined with &#96;LIMIT&#96; and &#96;OFFSET&#96;, MySQL can return duplicate rows across pages or skip rows entirely.[1][2][3] This occurs because when multiple rows have identical values in the &#96;ORDER BY&#96; column, MySQL considers the sort order of those rows as nondeterministic and may return them in different orders depending on the execution plan.[1]\n\n## Why This Happens\n\nThis is not a bug but an intentional MySQL optimization introduced in version 5.6.[1] According to MySQL's official documentation, when the &#96;ORDER BY&#96; clause doesn't guarantee a unique order, the result order is undefined, and MySQL is free to return rows in any order.[2][3] The presence of &#96;LIMIT&#96; can affect the execution plan, causing different row orders in successive queries.[1]\n\n## The Solution\n\n**Add a unique column to your ORDER BY clause** to make the sort order deterministic.[1][2] For example, instead of:\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nSELECT * FROM ratings ORDER BY category LIMIT 5;\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nUse:\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nSELECT * FROM ratings ORDER BY category, id LIMIT 5;\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis ensures consistent pagination results across all pages.[1]\n\n## Important Caveat\n\nEven with a deterministic &#96;ORDER BY&#96;, data modifications between page requests can cause duplicates or missing rows. If rows are inserted or deleted while paginating, subsequent pages may contain duplicates from the previous page or miss rows entirely.[2]",

```json
    }
```

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'll synthesize for the user:

- 1 - Issues with ORDER BY clauses in SQL systems can lead to undefined ordering when dealing with duplicate values, particularly with LIMIT/OFFSET or unstable keyset pagination.
- This results in inconsistent page boundaries where rows may be duplicated or skipped across pages.
- Such bugs are documented in MySQL community resources and represent known issues in SQL-backed systems.
- These problems aren't necessarily tied to Gravity Forms specifically but are common in any system using SQL pagination with duplicate values.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

It does **not** show up as a widely titled “Gravity Forms REST bug: missing entries” headline in what turned up in search, but the **behavior matches a very well known class of production bugs**: **paged queries ordered only by a non-unique column** (here `date_updated` with many identical values).

### What is “known”

**1. Database / SQL pagination (the real mechanism)**  
If the backend does something like `ORDER BY date_updated DESC` with `LIMIT`/`OFFSET` (or equivalent) and **does not add a unique tie-breaker** (e.g. `, id DESC`), the order among rows that share the same `date_updated` is **not guaranteed**. Across pages you can see **the same row on two pages** or **rows skipped**—depending on planner, buffer state, and version. That is discussed in the MySQL/SQL world as a **pagination + duplicate sort keys** problem, not as “random GF weirdness”:

- [Same record shows up multiple times in ordered paginated query](https://dba.stackexchange.com/questions/123737/same-record-shows-up-multiple-times-in-ordered-paginated-query) (dba.SE)  
- [MySQL paging sorting with data duplication / priority queue](https://sobyte.net/post/2021-09/mysql-paging-sorting-data-duplicates) (explains non-stable ordering with `LIMIT`)  
- MySQL bug reports around **`LIMIT` + `ORDER BY` + duplicate keys** (e.g. [bug #69732](https://bugs.mysql.com/bug.php?id=69732), [bug #116366](https://bugs.mysql.com/bug.php?id=116366)) describe overlapping/duplicate rows across pages in similar situations.

So it is **“too horrible” only if you assume every product adds a secondary sort key**; in practice many stacks get this wrong until someone hits bulk imports with identical timestamps.

**2. Gravity Forms side**  
Official REST docs describe **`sorting[key]` / `sorting[direction]` / `sorting[is_numeric]`** and explicitly show sorting by **`id`** ([Searching and Getting Entries with the REST API v2](https://docs.gravityforms.com/searching-and-getting-entries-with-the-rest-api-v2/)). That is consistent with “**stable sort is your job**”: `date_updated` alone is a poor pagination key when ties are dense (GravityView batch imports are exactly that).

There is also older chatter about **REST paging parameters** being fiddly (e.g. [Stack Overflow: Gravity Forms REST API paging](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/59272905/gravity-forms-rest-api-paging-not-working)), but that is more about **how** to pass paging than about tie-breaking.

**3. Why it felt “impossible” in prod**  
- Admin CSV export / single-entry `GET /entries/{id}` can be **correct** while **paged list** is wrong—different code paths.  
- Default “newest first” (`date_updated` DESC) is natural for incremental syncs but **bad** for exhaustive paging without a tie-breaker.  
- Until bulk operations create hundreds of identical timestamps, the bug **stays latent**.

### Bottom line

Treat it as **known unstable pagination when ordering by a non-unique timestamp**, which Gravity Forms exposes but does not magically fix for you. Your switch to **list by `id`** is exactly the standard mitigation (and aligns with GF’s own REST examples). If you want a smoking gun in their tracker, you’d grep **Gravity Forms GitHub** / support for “REST entries pagination” + “duplicate” / “missing” + “sort”; the underlying issue is still the **SQL tie** problem above.
